questions.question: Which of the following is a natural magnet?
questions.explanation: Lodestone (also called magnetite, Fe₃O₄) is a naturally occurring magnetic iron ore. It was the first known natural magnet discovered by humans.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a magnetic material?
questions.explanation: Iron, cobalt, and nickel are the three main magnetic elements that are strongly attracted to magnets. Copper is a non-magnetic material.

questions.question: When the North pole of one magnet is brought near the South pole of another magnet, they will:
questions.explanation: The fundamental law of magnetism states that unlike poles (North and South) attract each other, while like poles (North-North or South-South) repel.

questions.question: The end of a freely suspended bar magnet that points towards the Earth's geographic North is called the:
questions.explanation: The pole that points toward the Earth's geographic North is called the North-seeking pole (or simply the North pole of the magnet). It is attracted to the Earth's magnetic South pole, which is located near the geographic North Pole.

questions.question: What is magnetic induction?
options: The science of producing electricity by using a magnet
A material becoming magnetised when placed near a permanent magnet
The practice of heating a magnet to very high temperature to increase its strength
Passing current through a wire to produce a magnetic field
questions.explanation: Magnetic induction is the process by which a magnetic material (like iron or steel) becomes a temporary magnet when placed in the magnetic field of a permanent magnet, without any physical contact between them.

questions.question: Which of the following is an artificial magnet?
questions.explanation: Artificial magnets are man-made, typically produced by magnetising materials like steel or iron using electric current or stroking methods. Lodestone and magnetite are naturally occurring magnets.

questions.question: A bar magnet is broken into three pieces. How many poles will each piece have?
questions.explanation: Each broken piece of a magnet becomes a complete magnet with both a North and a South pole. Magnetic monopoles (isolated single poles) do not exist.

questions.question: Which of the following retains magnetism for a longer time after being magnetised?
questions.explanation: Steel has high retentivity, meaning it retains magnetism for a long time after the magnetising force is removed. Soft iron has low retentivity and is used for temporary magnets like electromagnet cores.

questions.question: A freely suspended bar magnet always comes to rest in which direction?
questions.explanation: A freely suspended bar magnet aligns itself with the Earth's magnetic field, coming to rest approximately along the North-South direction. This is the working principle of a magnetic compass.

questions.question: Which of the following is a use of magnets? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY
questions.explanation: All of these are practical applications of magnets. Compass needles use magnetised pointers to align with Earth's magnetic field. Credit card magnetic strips store data on a magnetic stripe. Loudspeakers use a permanent magnet and an electromagnet to vibrate the cone and produce sound. Generators use rotating magnets (or coils) and electromagnetic induction to produce electricity. Dynamic microphones use magnets to convert sound waves into electrical signals.

questions.question: The force of attraction of a magnet is strongest at its:
questions.explanation: The magnetic force is concentrated at the poles (North and South) of a magnet, where the magnetic field lines are densest. The field is weakest at the centre (middle) of the magnet.

questions.question: If you bring the North pole of a bar magnet near an unmagnetised iron nail, the end of the nail closest to the magnet becomes:
options: First a North pole, then a South pole
questions.explanation: By magnetic induction, the end of the iron nail nearest to the North pole of the magnet becomes an induced South pole (unlike pole). This is why attraction occurs — unlike poles attract.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have a strong grasp of the fundamentals of magnetism!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review magnetic induction and the properties of magnetic poles.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the law of magnetic poles, magnetic materials, and the concept of magnetic induction.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: natural vs artificial magnets, magnetic vs non-magnetic materials, poles, and how a compass works.
